name: Walleye
tell: White tip on the lower fork of the tail, the giveaway
Glassy, reflective eyes and golden flanks
Two separate dorsal fins with sharp spines up front
eat: Yellow perch, minnows, leeches and mayfly larvae, mostly at dusk and after dark
bite: Jig and minnow, worm harnesses, crankbaits along drop-offs in low light

name: Sauger
tell: Like a small walleye but no white tail tip
Dark saddle blotches down the back
Rows of black dots across the spiny dorsal fin
eat: Small fish and insect larvae, usually deeper and murkier water than walleye
bite: Small jigs tipped with minnows, fished slow near bottom

name: Largemouth bass
tell: Jaw reaches past the back of the eye
Dark, broken stripe along the side
Deep notch between the two dorsal fins
eat: Bluegill, crayfish, frogs, and anything that fits in the bucket mouth
bite: Soft plastics, spinnerbaits, and topwater frogs around weeds and docks

name: Smallmouth bass
tell: Jaw ends under the eye, not past it
Bronze body with faint vertical bars
Red eyes on most fish, shallow dorsal notch
eat: Crayfish first, then gobies and minnows over rock and gravel
bite: Tube jigs, ned rigs and drop shots on rocky points and shoals

name: Northern pike
tell: Light bean shaped spots in horizontal rows on a dark body
Rounded tail lobes
Five or fewer pores under each side of the jaw
eat: Whatever swims by: fish, frogs, even the odd duckling
bite: Spoons, big spinners and live bait under a float near weed edges

name: Muskellunge
tell: Dark bars or spots on a light body, the reverse of pike
Markings run vertically where pike run horizontal
Pointed tail lobes and six to nine pores under each jaw side
eat: Big fish, and occasionally muskrats and waterbirds
bite: Large bucktails and jerkbaits, with a figure eight at the boat every cast

name: Yellow perch
tell: Six to eight dark vertical bars on gold
Orange lower fins, brightest on males
Two separate dorsal fins like a tiny walleye
eat: Insect larvae, small minnows and young crayfish
bite: Small jigs or worms under a float, and the classic through the ice

name: Crappie
tell: Speckled calico pattern over silver
Deep, flat body with a paper thin mouth
Dorsal and anal fins nearly mirror each other
eat: Minnows, zooplankton and insects, feeding up in the water column
bite: Tiny tube jigs or a minnow under a float around brush and cribs

name: Bluegill and pumpkinseed
tell: Pancake shaped, palm sized panfish
Bluegill: plain dark ear flap and a dusky blotch at the rear of the dorsal
Pumpkinseed: red spot on the ear flap and wavy blue cheek lines
eat: Insects, snails and larvae picked from weeds and bottom
bite: A piece of worm under a bobber does it every time

name: Rock bass
tell: Red eyes
Rows of dark dots along the flanks
Bigger mouth than the other sunfish, six spines in the anal fin
eat: Crayfish, insects and small minnows around rock and timber
bite: Small crankbaits, jigs and worms near rocky cover

name: Brook trout
tell: Light worm like squiggles across the back
Red spots with blue halos on the sides
White leading edges on the lower fins
eat: Insects on top and below, plus small minnows in cold, clean water
bite: Small spinners, worms and flies in creeks and spring fed lakes

name: Brown trout
tell: Golden brown with black and red spots, many with pale halos
Tail mostly free of spots
Square tail and a thick wrist ahead of it
eat: Insects, minnows and crayfish, hunting hardest at dusk and dark
bite: Spinners, minnow baits and flies, especially in the evening

name: Rainbow trout
tell: Pink band down the side
Small black spots over the whole body and the entire tail
Silver in the big lakes, where it goes by steelhead
eat: Insects, eggs and small minnows, on the move in current
bite: Roe bags, small spinners and flies in the rivers, spoons on the lakes

name: Lake trout
tell: Deeply forked tail
Cream spots over dark grey or green
White edges on the lower fins, no pink band ever
eat: Cisco, smelt and whitefish in deep, cold water
bite: Deep spoons and tube jigs, or trolling until the graph lights up

name: Splake
tell: A hatchery cross of brook and lake trout
Some brook trout squiggles but a partly forked tail
Mostly in stocked lakes, so the stocking list is your best clue
eat: Minnows and insects, like both parents
bite: Small spoons and jigs, a favourite through the ice

name: Atlantic salmon
tell: Black X shaped spots on silver
No spots on the tail
Slim tail wrist you can lift it by, unlike a brown trout
eat: Baitfish and drifting insects
bite: Flies and small spoons, most famously in the restored Lake Ontario runs

name: Chinook and coho salmon
tell: Chinook: black gums and spots on both tail lobes
Coho: white gums, black tongue, spots on the upper lobe only
Both turn dark and hook jawed for the fall run
eat: Alewife and smelt out in the big lake
bite: Spoons and flasher and fly off downriggers, roe in the rivers

name: Channel catfish
tell: Deeply forked tail, the only Ontario catfish with one
Curved anal fin and scattered spots on younger fish
Eight barbels and not a scale on it
eat: Nearly anything on bottom: fish, crayfish, mussels and worms
bite: Cut bait, chicken liver or a glob of worms on bottom after dark

name: Lake whitefish
tell: Small mouth overhung by a blunt snout
Bright silver with big smooth scales
Forked tail and a little adipose fin like a trout
eat: Insect larvae, snails and freshwater shrimp off the bottom
bite: Small jigs and spoons worked near bottom, big through the ice

name: Cisco (lake herring)
tell: Mouth at the tip of the snout, not overhung like a whitefish
Slimmer and softer looking, with a large eye
Travels in big midwater schools
eat: Plankton, almost entirely
bite: Tiny jigs and spoons in winter, small flies in the fall schools

name: Lake sturgeon
tell: Shark like tail and rows of bony plates
Four barbels ahead of a vacuum hose mouth
Ontario's biggest and oldest fish, over a century sometimes
eat: Insect larvae, snails and crayfish vacuumed off the bottom
bite: Season is closed in most zones, so check your zone before anything else
